div.div: DANGER: Do not use this file for input. Use the designated files for testing the package. That has the same terms and relationships without the guide text.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 298px; height: 1px; padding-top: 194px; margin-left: 197px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 131px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.p: You can declare domains and ranges using the original RFDS object properties.
Nested collections are now natively supported to allow for complex domain-range constructs.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 148px; height: 1px; padding-top: 687px; margin-left: 521px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: You can use draw.io lists to create complex nested collections used in OWL turtle syntax.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 428px; height: 1px; padding-top: 249px; margin-left: 1042px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 166px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.p: Relationships between classes and instances now include declaration of equivalence and disjointness.
These statements are usually added for aligning ontologies, or creating complex statements about the nature of a class or object.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 148px; height: 1px; padding-top: 357px; margin-left: 2331px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: The classes used on the left are declared here with their hierarchies for context.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 148px; height: 1px; padding-top: 467px; margin-left: 3001px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: Properties can also have hierarchies albeit at more limited logical expressiveness.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 528px; height: 1px; padding-top: 969px; margin-left: 20px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 96px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.p: Datatypes are now officially supported alongside their with and inside in nested collections. Datatypes can also be used in domain and range definitions for datatype properties.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 98px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1462px; margin-left: 351px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: Literals can also be used in collections for things like datatype enumerations.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 98px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1602px; margin-left: 21px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: As mentioned, datatypes can also be placed inside nested collections.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 148px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1552px; margin-left: 1012px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: XSD datatypes are supported, including turtle-based input formats for complicated types like duration.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 568px; height: 1px; padding-top: 999px; margin-left: 1362px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 126px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.div: The package supports all axioms and restrictions that can be described in the Manchester syntax (MS), except for deep restriction nesting, language ranges and the NOT construct. The package features a hybrid visual-first translation of MS concepts like restrictions, logical operations, property chain axioms and datatype facets.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 598px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1739px; margin-left: 42px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 96px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.p: Classes can now be defined in terms of enumerations, or lists of things that strictly compose a given class, object or concept. They use the list object from draw.io like other nested collections.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 118px; height: 1px; padding-top: 2172px; margin-left: 981px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: In this case, Golf Tour Champion can only have the listed player instances as members.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 188px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1370px; margin-left: 1621px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: Unlike windows and frames in MS, a restriction is a head node for the axiom/restriction statement. All restrictions start with owl:Restriction header. Basic concepts like domains, ranges, and class equivalence don't need owl:Restriction

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 188px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1577px; margin-left: 1276px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: The hybrid syntax comes from using turtle terms to define the syntactic declaration of restrictions; for instance, onProperty to refer to the property restrictions on a class.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 188px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1788px; margin-left: 1201px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: CEMENTO defines shorthand notation for MS terms. The package comes with an ontology file that contains all translated concepts from turtle to MS.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 188px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1842px; margin-left: 2352px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: A user is also able to define datatype facets. Instead of complicated nested turtle, datatypes can now be defined with the logically consistent MS bracket notation.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 118px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1907px; margin-left: 1691px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: A user can utilize nested collections inside restrictions, enabling the composition of several elements of logic into one cohesive diagram.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 168px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1703px; margin-left: 1681px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: Each restriction is treated like a logic stream, in which one branch is a complete statement about that particular restriction.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 228px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1517px; margin-left: 1562px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: Restriction branches always come from the same restriction head node. Branches that share a node are logically combined with the AND operator (i.e. owl:intersectionOf)

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 191px; height: 1px; padding-top: 2067px; margin-left: 1835px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div:
This translates to the MS statement:
Types: hasWife min 1 HappyPerson

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe center; width: 128px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1912px; margin-left: 1951px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: center; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: The term ms:of is used when chaining literals to classes.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 258px; height: 1px; padding-top: 954px; margin-left: 2432px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 126px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.div: Some classes might be defined by the behavior of their instances. RDF has the subproperty chain concept for this use case, and CEMENTO allows users to draw-up these restrictions with ease.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 228px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1476px; margin-left: 2642px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: The package uses a custom ms:subPropertyChain term for declaring property chains.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe center; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 138px; height: 1px; padding-top: 1618px; margin-left: 2642px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;

div.div: The ms:o is used to chain one property to another in a property chain.

div.style: display: flex; align-items: unsafe flex-start; justify-content: unsafe flex-start; width: 568px; height: 1px; padding-top: 954px; margin-left: 2937px;
box-sizing: border-box; font-size: 0; text-align: left; max-height: 126px; overflow: hidden; color: #000000;
display: inline-block; font-size: 12px; font-family: Helvetica; color: light-dark(#000000, #ffffff); line-height: 1.2; pointer-events: all; white-space: normal; word-wrap: normal;
div.div: CEMENTO makes it easier to declare logical combinations (AND, OR) by separating the operator from the restriction branches. Users can "nest" or bridge several restrictions together in a manner consistent with the streams analogy.
